runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) taxable income elasticity survey
Keane (2011) labor supply review
EITC/lone-mother labor supply literature as a low-income intensive/extensive-margin anchor
runs.reasoningSummary: My memory is that primary earners typically have modest substitution elasticities, generally below secondary earners, often around 0.1–0.3 in structural tax-benefit settings. For decile 1 primary earners, I shade lower-to-middle in that range because intensive-margin hours responses are limited, while low-income workers may still be somewhat responsive to effective marginal wage changes under tax-benefit phase-ins and phase-outs.

For decile 1 primary earners, I shade lower-to-middle in that range because intensive-margin hours responses are limited, while low-income workers may still be somewhat responsive to effective marginal wage changes under tax-benefit phase-ins and phase-outs."}
